GRADY
It's Mrs. Gaskell's. Her hobby.

I thought you were Mrs. Gaskell ' s hobby,
Tripp.
GRADY
Piss off, Crabs. I lost a wife today.
CRABTREE
Oh, I' m sure you ' ll find another. She ' ll
be young, beautiful. They alwa y s are.
EXT. FRONT PORCH - GASKELL HOUSE
As t he front door swings open, Sara Gaskell appears , r i ding a
wave of jagged party CHATTER onto the porch.
SARA
Well, hello, everyone. Terry, good to see
you again.
CRABTREE
Chancello r . Don ' t you look ravishing.
SARA
Aren't you sweet to say so . I was
beginning to wonder if you were ever
going to--oh!
As Sara s t eps forward, her heel ca t ches and she p itc hes
forward ... into Grady's arms.

GRADY
Ask him if he's conflicted abou t his
house in the Hamptons.
HANNAH GREEN
(brightening)
Grady.
GRADY
Q, Hannah's had two stories published in
The Paris YolRatbeetwdust off the
'A ' material for her .
You didn't tell me you were a writer.
HANNAH GREEN
You didn't ask .
As Grady moves off, he sees Poe sniffing, and goes the other
way, heading directly into the crosshairs of a MAN IN TWEED,
who i s talking to another, shorte r MAN.
GRADY
Hello, Nathan.
MAN IN TWEED
Grady ! My God, :I haven' t seen you since,
what? The PEN/Faulkner Awards . -That was a
big night for you.

MISS SLOVIAK
I need a ride .
GRADY
I'm your man.
EXT. STREET
As the Galaxie's big trunk yawns open, Mi ss Sloviak stares at what ' s
wedged up against her suitcase.
GRADY
There's an explanation.
INT. GRADY' S CAR - MOVING
As Grady drives, Miss Sloviak finishes with t he top button of
a man ' s shirt, then reaches into the zippered COSMETICS BAG
in her lap. Onto the open tray of the glovebox, she places a

MISS SLOVIAK
Couldn't he have just thrown a shoe at
the poor thing?
GRADY
Jame s is ... I don't know ...
MISS SLOVIAK
Disturbed . And when your friend Crabtree
gets done with him , he's going to be even

CONTINUED: ( 3 )
CRABTREE
Uh uh. A jockey. His name"s, um,
Curtis; .. curtis Hardapple.
GRADY
Not Curtis.
CRABTREE
Vernon, then. Vernon Hardapple. The
scar's are from a - -from a horse . He fell
during a race and got trampled.
GRADY
And now he's addicted to painkillers.
CRABTREE
He can't p{ss standing up anymore.
GRADY
He lives with his mother.
CRABTREE
And he's got a younger brother
who .. . was ... a ...

Groom . Named Claudelle. And his mother
blames Vernon f or his death.

Grady tosses the bag into Crabtree, waves his hand wearily to

GRADY
Go home to your mother, Vernon.
Terry and Vernon exchange looks.
VERNON HARDAPPLE
What are you lookin g at?
CRABTREE
I'm lookin g at you, fella.
(to Grady)
Come on, . killjoy, go.
GRADY
(sensing Crabtree's grumpiness)
Come on, Crabs, the kid was practically
in a coma.
CRABTREE
And who's fault do you suppose that would
be?
GRADY
I was trying to calm him do¼lil;
CRABTREE
Oh, you calmed him down, Dr. Fee lgood .

TRAXLER
Say, Professor Tripp, is all that stuff
true about Errol Flynn? How he used to
put paprika on his dick. To make it, yo u
know, like more stimu lating for the
chick?
GRADY
Chr i st, Traxler . How the hell should I
know?
TRAXLER
You're reading his biography, aren ' t you?
Sam points and Grady glances at t he knapsack riding on the
seat between him and Sam. A BOOK--bearing ERROL FLYNN'S
PICTURE--is tucked into the side pouch.
GRADY
Oh, right. Yeah, that's true. He used to
rub all kinds of things on it. Salad
dressing . Ground lamb.
TRAXLER
Sick.
EXT . GASKELL' S HOUSE - NIGHT (MOMENTS LATER)
Sam brings the car to a coughing idle across the street from
the Gas kell's house.

He crosses to a high table, sets the balloon down, and steps
back , considering the placement.
SARA (OS)
Feeling guilty?
Grady jumps--startled- - and turns. Sara has materialized
behind a ficus, large POTTING GLOVES on her hands.
SARA (cont'd )
I can't believe you hung up on me, yo u
dick.
GRADY
Sorry. A lot was happening this morning.
Can you talk?
Sara nods, moves the ficus to another table.
SARA
Walter's a basket case. Someone stole Marilyn 's
jacket last night. And Poe's missing, too.
GRADY
I heard .
SARA
You heard? How?
GRADY
A twelve-year-old policeman carne by the
hou se this morning.
SARA
Did you confess?

(moving off)
Make yourself at home.
INT. UPSTAIRS BEDROOM
Grady, labor i ng badly on his ankle, enters and finds a DUFFEL
BAG open on the floor, its contents a tangle of quickly ­
packed clothes. Everything else in the room feels of another
time. PHOTOGRAPHS are everywhere, documenting a PRETTY GIRL'S
life, from first recital to cap and gowri.
One photograph lies face down. Grady turns it over and finds jthe
pretty girl grown into a beautiful young woman, standing in a white
gown next to a younger Grady- - on their wedding day.
INT. DEN
James enters and sees a REMOTE CONTROL atop the BAR. Taking it, he
points it at the WIDE- SCREEN TELEVISION imbedded in the opposite
wall and, seconds lat er , GEORGE SANDERS walks into his CLOSE-UP.
GEORGE SANDERS
There's no such thing as a good
influence, Mr . Gray. All influenc e is
immora l .. .
James pours himself a tumbler of Bushmills.
GEORGE SANDERS (CONT'D)
There 's onl y one way to get rid of
temptation, and that's to yield to it .

GRADY
Carl?
{shakin g his head)
Never mind. Just get us a tabl e .
James doesn't move, his face a pale moon in the icy light of
the par king lot.
GRADY (cont'd )
I 'm not going to let the most talented
writer in my class eat some week-ol d
chee se sandwich.
James glances up, uncertain.
GRADY (cont'd)
And I ' m not going to l et him sleep on any
bus bench ei ther.
As James' face lights up, Grady looks away, waves him insid e.
GRADY (cont'd)
Go on. I' l l be ther e in a minute .
As James shuffles inside, Gra dy l ets the door close and
watch es a big REDHEAD in a waitress cap l ead James to a booth
wi th a v i ew of the highway. Fina lly , a s if concl uding some
CONTINUED: ( 2)
internal deba te, Grady heads for the PAYPHONE shimmering
across the way .
IN/EXT. PHONE BOOTH - PARKING LOT - MOMENTS LATER
Grady rests his forehead against the PAYPHONE as he speaks.

But I had decided to rescue James Leer. I
wasn't quite sure from what, because I
was pretty much convinced that everything
that came out of James' mouth was
complete and utter horse shit.
Grady--limping like an aging prizefighter- -leads Crabtree up
a steep incline toward an enormous three-story house. The
Leer's Mercedes gleams in the shadows.
CRABTREE
Yikes. There must be two dozen windows on that
thing. How are we supposed to find his?
GRADY
I told you. They keep him chained in the
basement. Come on.
EXT. REAR - LEER HOUSE
LIGHT GLOWS from a low BASEMENT WINDOW. From inside, a WOMAN
is HEARD SINGING. Grady .and Crabtree pause, listen .
WOMAN SINGING
Unrequited love's a bore
And I've got it pretty bad
But for someone you adore
It's a pleasure to be sad
CRABTREE
Rodgers and Ha rt.
Crabtree and Grady look at each other .

GOATEE KID
Uh, Pro fessor Tripp ? You know, last
semester, what I said that time in offi ce
hours--! hope there ' s no hard feelings.
GRADY
(no idea what he's tal king
about)
No . ..
GOATEE KID
I mean, I was breaki ng up with this g irl
at the t ime and my car was all fucked up
and--well --I was pretty bent in general.
GRADY
It ' s cool, Jeff. Real ly.
GOATEE KID
I just want you t o know that's why I dropp ed you r
class and sai d all that shit about the university
stealing my money and you bei ng a ps eudo­
Faulknerian nobody.
EXT. PORCH - GRADY'S HOUSE - NI GHT (A BIT LATER )
Grady sits quietly on the porch steps, a j oint burning i n his
fingers. To his left, perc hed atop a Haage n- Daz s container,
is a TELEPHONE. As he pulls it into his lap, a LAMP in the

SHATTERS on the floor .

CRABTREE
The Chancellor's here? Now?
CONTINUED: ( 2 )
,GRADY
Evidently .
(calling )
Coming!
JAMES LEER
Does she--I rnean--does she know
about .. . her dog?
GRADY
It's Walter's dog and yes, s he does.
Know. But let ' s spare her the detai l s.
Come on, your shoes are in the hall.
CRABTREE
James. This book of yours. It's not bad.
Not bad at a l l.
James stops, considers this piece of news with a look of deep
seriousness, then nods.
JAMES LEER
Thank you.
CRABTREE
You 're welcome.
As James shu ffl es off into the ha ll , Crabtree looks at Grady,
his eye s dancing with excitement.

CRABTREE
So whose car is i t?
GRADY
My guess--Vernon Hardapple.
CRABTREE
The hood jump e r?
GRADY
He said a few things that lead me to believe the

CRABTREE
Such as .
GRADY
'That's my car, motherfucker.'
CRABTREE
Uh huh. So. We find Verno n , we find the

GRADY
.. . we find the jacket.
CRABTREE
There's only one problem, Tripp. We don't
know his real name. We just made it up.
In fact, we made the whole guy up.

INT. SARA'S OFFICE - CONTINUOUS
Gr ady eases open a door with Rm. 707, CHANCELLOR s t encile d on
the glass and steps into an outer offic e. WordFest f l yers,

Throughout--perched atop filing cabinets, the desk itself-­

The larger room beyond--Sara's office-- i s empty.
INT. BACK ROW - AUDITORIUM - THAW HALL
Grady enters, picks his way behind the back row, scanning the
aisles for Sara .
GIRL
Hey, Professor Tripp.
It's Carrie McWhirty, James ' tormentor from workshop. Grady
takes another look around, then drops i nto the seat next to

CARRIE MCWHIRTY
If you're l ooking for Hannah, she"s on
the aisle.
GRADY
No . ..
But Grady looks anyway. Hannah si ts a dozen rows down the
aisle, hair pulled back in a clip, glorious ski n gl eami ng.
The Goatee Kid sits close beside her.

GRADY
(pushing past him)
Thanks, Q. I fel t the same way about the
bank teller's breasts in your last one.
INT. CORRIDOR - THAW HALL - MOMENTS LATER
Grady bursts into the corridor.
GRADY
Sara!
Its empty. Quiet. Grady pauses. Somewhere, a HEAVY METAL DOOR
CLOSES. Grady rushes on.
INT. NEW CORRIDOR
Grady, limping badly, turns a corner and sees a DOOR. He
moves to it, pushes past ...
INT. STAIRWELL - CONTINUOUS
. .. and finds himself standing in a stairwell. He lean s out
over the railing, peers down. It's a steep drop , very steep,
ending in a small rectangular space, a kind of basement
of f ice, with VENDING MACHINES, PLASTIC CHAIRS, and a
COLLAPSIBLE CARD TABLE.
